content:
Hey Ryan,

I feel the weight of what you’re asking. There really can be a social cost here—being thought anti‑science, making your kids field awkward questions, feeling “fringe.” We shouldn’t pretend that doesn’t matter. At the same time, we also don’t steer our convictions by what plays well in the room. Scripture calls us to “fear God, not man,” and to carry the reproach of Christ when needed. Both realities are true.

A couple of guiding principles that help me:

- Inerrancy is about what Scripture actually intends to affirm. The Bible speaks truly in the sense the authors meant to convey, using the genres and everyday speech they used. It’s full of phenomenological language—“sunrise,” “ends of the earth,” “foundations”—which are not scientific claims but human vantage‑point descriptions. That’s not a dodge; it’s part of taking the text seriously on its own terms.

- “Literal” doesn’t mean “wooden.” The literal sense is the literary/authorial sense. Poetry isn’t read like a lab report; narrative isn’t read like apocalyptic. Holding to inerrancy doesn’t force us to flatten genres.

- Don’t add offense the gospel doesn’t require. Paul was willing to be thought a fool for Christ (1 Cor. 1:18), yet also worked hard to remove unnecessary stumbling blocks (1 Cor. 9:19–23). We should bear the right costs, not self‑inflicted ones.

What about the “firmament”? The Hebrew raqia can be translated “expanse” (many modern translations do). In the ancient Near Eastern setting, people often imagined the sky with imagery that sounds like something stretched out or beaten out. Genesis uses that language. Faithful, inerrantist readers land in a few different places:

- Some take raqia as a straightforward reference to the sky/space/atmosphere from an earthbound vantage point, without intending a material dome claim.

- Some think Moses is accommodating familiar imagery to describe God’s ordering of creation, not teaching a cosmological blueprint.

- A smaller number do argue for a hard‑surface reading. Even then, they’re usually not saying Genesis is a science text, but that God used real, ordinary‑language descriptions that the first audience would grasp.

My point: you don’t have to stake Christian orthodoxy—or your kids’ peace of mind—on a specific physical model of the sky. What Scripture clearly and centrally teaches in Genesis is that God alone created, ordered, and blessed the world; humanity bears His image; creation is good; Sabbath rest crowns it. Those are hills to die on. Whether raqia must be a solid dome is not.

Is God big enough to be honored either way? Yes—if what you hold, you hold in faith, with a sincere desire to submit to His Word, not simply to avoid embarrassment. The issue isn’t God’s “bigness”; it’s our faithfulness. Two people can land differently on raqia and both honor God if they are honestly trying to hear what He said. And two people can land the same way for opposite reasons—one out of reverence for Scripture, the other out of fear of man. God cares about both truth and motive.

A few heart checks I use:
- Am I letting the text set the terms, or my tribe, my anxiety, or the zeitgeist?
- Have I listened humbly to the best arguments from faithful scholars who love Scripture?
- Does my view force me to deny clear teachings elsewhere, or is it a secondary matter where Romans 14 charity applies?

Practically, with your kids or skeptical friends, you might say: “I believe the Bible is true in everything God intends to teach. Genesis tells us who made the world and why; it uses ordinary, ancient ways of speaking about the sky. I don’t think the Bible is giving a physics lesson there. I’m not anti‑science; I’m pro‑truth, and I don’t want to make the Bible say what it never meant to say.” That’s a faithful, inerrantist posture that also lowers the temperature.

So no, we shouldn’t insist on a particular “literal firmament” reading if the text itself doesn’t require it. That would be adding a yoke Scripture doesn’t place. But if after careful study your conscience is persuaded a more rigid reading is what Moses intended, then bear any social cost with quiet courage and kindness—without making it a test of fellowship.

God will honor those who tremble at His Word (Isa. 66:2). Aim there. Hold the center tightly, hold secondary matters with humility, and don’t confuse fidelity with needless contrarianism. I’m with you in it.
